journal-title-group.journal-title: Clinical cancer research : an official journal of the American Association for Cancer Research

title-group.article-title: Azacitidine, Venetoclax and Magrolimab in Newly Diagnosed and Relapsed Refractory Acute Myeloid Leukemia: Phase 1b/2 Study and Correlative Analysis

article-meta.aff: 1Department of Leukemia, The University of Texas, MD Anderson Cancer Center, Houston
2Department of Cancer Medicine, The University of Texas, MD Anderson Cancer Center, Houston
3Department of Hematopathology, The University of Texas, MD Anderson Cancer Center, Houston
4Department of Biostatistics, The University of Texas, MD Anderson Cancer Center, Houston
5Department of Genomic Medicine, The University of Texas, MD Anderson Cancer Center, Houston

fn.p: ND designed and supervised the study. JS wrote the first draft of the manuscript. ND, JS, HMK, PR, MY, CDD, TMK, AM, GMB, GB, KS, NP, GCI, MA, FR, GGM, HAA provided patients. JS, JE curated the data. JS, JN analyzed the data. JS, SA made the clinical figures. SL, GT provided clinical laboratory data. BW, PKR, MYY, HAA performed the correlative analysis. HAA designed and supervised the correlative analysis. All authors reviewed and approved the final version of the manuscript.

author-notes.corresp: Corresponding authors: Naval Daver, MD, Professor, Department of Leukemia, The University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center, Houston, Texas 77030, ndaver@mdanderson.org, Hussein A. Abbas, MD, PhD, Assistant Professor, Department of Leukemia, The University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center, Houston, Texas 77030, habbas@mdanderson.org

license.license-p: This file is available for text mining. It may also be used consistent with the principles of fair use under the copyright law.

sec.title: Purpose
sec.p: Magrolimab is a monoclonal antibody directed against macrophage checkpoint CD47 on myeloid leukemia cells that was pre-clinically synergistic with azacitidine-venetoclax, warranting further clinical evaluation.

sec.title: Patients and Methods:
sec.p: In this phase 1b/2 study the triplet combination of azacitidine, venetoclax and magrolimab was evaluated in adult patients with frontline (ineligible for intensive chemotherapy) and relapsed/refractory AML. Azacitidine was dosed at 75mg/m2 for 7 days, venetoclax at 400 mg/day for 28 days, and magrolimab (recommended phase 2 dose [RP2D]) as follows: 1 mg/kg dose on days 1 and 4, 15 mg/kg on day 8, 30 mg/kg on day 11, 15 and 22 (cycle 1), followed by 30 mg/kg weekly for cycle 2, then 30 mg/kg every 2 weeks cycle 3 and beyond. The primary endpoint was RP2D for phase 1b and rates of composite complete response (CRc) in phase 2.

sec.title: Results:
sec.p: The frontline cohort included 54 patients (median age 70.1 years); 35 (64.8%) were TP53 mutated (TP53mut). CRc was attained in 34 patients (63%); 49% in TP53mut and 90% in the TP53 wild-type patients. At a median follow-up of 27.9 months, the median event free survival (EFS) and overall survival (OS) was 6.6 months and 9.8 months respectively; for TP53mut patients the median EFS and OS was 5.9 and 7.6 months, while for TP53 wild type it was 9.6 months and 13 months respectively. CRc in the relapsed/refractory cohort (n=52) was 29% and median OS was 3.9 months. The regimen was well tolerated; infections were the most common ≥ grade 3 adverse event (75.4%) with no immune toxicities or deaths related to therapy. scRNAseq was performed on 27 longitudinal samples from 11 TP53mut patients (8 responders). Gene set enrichment analysis revealed enrichment of IFNγ and TNFα signaling in non-responders at baseline, while erythroid differentiation was associated with resistance. Patients at relapse also showed up-regulated CD47 expression and elevated leukemia regeneration score.

sec.title: Conclusions:
sec.p: The triplet regimen was safe but did not lead to promising survival outcomes.

sec.title: Introduction:
sec.p: Azacitidine and venetoclax improved the outcomes of older and unfit patients with newly diagnosed acute myeloid leukemia (AML), however in patients with high-risk AML harboring complex cytogenetics with or without a TP53 aberration, outcomes remained dismal1–5. With intensive chemotherapy median survival in AML with TP53 aberrations is 5–8 months across most studies6,7. Adding novel agents with efficacy against myeloid leukemia cells to azacitidine and venetoclax based on established preclinical synergy and with careful dose optimization, is a key investigational approach to improve outcomes in those with genomically high-risk disease8.

An important ongoing arena of oncology drug development includes harnessing the innate immune system against cancer cells9. CD47 is a surface protein ubiquitously expressed on myeloid leukemia cells that inhibits the activity of macrophages and other phagocytes by binding to signal regulatory protein alpha10,11. Preclinical studies demonstrated that inhibiting CD47 led to increased phagocytic leukemic cell death12. Magrolimab (Hu5F9-G4) is an anti-CD47 IgG4 monoclonal antibody which in early-phase single arm clinical trials for patients with AML and myelodysplastic syndrome (MDS) was found to be tolerable in combination with azacitidine and other than on target manageable anemia, did not show major drug-related safety concerns13,14. In the recently reported phase 1b study of magrolimab in combination with azacitidine in 87 older/unfit patients with previously untreated AML, the combination led to a complete remission (CR) rate of 32% (CR/CR with incomplete count recovery [CRi] rate of 39%) for the full patient cohort and 32% (CR/CRi rate of 40%) for the TP53 mutated (TP53mut) cohort. The median overall survival (OS) in the TP53 wild-type and mutated patients were 18.9 and 9.8 months, respectively13. Furthermore the addition of magrolimab to azacitidine and venetoclax (AVM triplet) was synergistic in preclinical studies involving AML cell lines and patient derived xenograft (PDX) models, even mitigating venetoclax resistance15. This led to efforts to combine azacitidine-venetoclax with magrolimab in clinic with our study reported here being the first of such efforts. The phase 3 randomized ENHANCE-3 study comparing magrolimab or placebo added to azacitidine and venetoclax in patients with newly diagnosed AML was stopped early for futility and possible excess adverse events (AEs) in the magrolimab arm; detailed results are awaited16. Similarly, the phase 3 randomized ENHANCE 2 trial comparing azacitidine and magrolimab to azacitidine and venetoclax (intensive chemotherapy ineligible) or 7+3 chemotherapy (intensive chemotherapy eligible) in adult patients with untreated TP53mut AML was also stopped early for futility17,18.

Herein, we present the final update of the phase 1b/2 open-label predecessor clinical trial of AVM in patients with newly diagnosed and relapsed/refractory (R/R) AML (NCT04435691). In a subset of newly diagnosed AML patients harboring TP53 alterations, we longitudinally characterized the mechanisms of resistance and response to the triplet therapy using single cell RNA (scRNA) profiling to derive intrinsic and extrinsic characteristics associated with responses.

sec.title: Study design and patients
sec.p: This single center phase 1b/2 clinical trial included patients with newly diagnosed or R/R AML. The phase 1b dose finding phase allowed patients ≥18 years of age with ≤2 prior lines of AML therapy, not immediately eligible for allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT) and Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group performance status (ECOG-PS) of ≤2. The phase 2 portion allowed enrollment after establishment of the recommended phase 2 dose (RP2D) from the phase 1b.

The phase 2 frontline cohort allowed patients who were ineligible for intensive chemotherapy because of age ≥75 years or <75 years with ECOG-PS of 2 or significant comorbidities and documented organ dysfunction (supplement [S] protocol, Section 4.1.3). Additionally, patients ≥18 years of age with newly diagnosed AML with adverse risk karyotype as per the European LeukemiaNet (ELN) 2017 recommendations and/or with TP53 mutations/deletions could be enrolled on the frontline cohort regardless of their eligibility for intensive chemotherapy18.

Patients who had received prior therapy for MDS, chronic myelomonocytic leukemia (CMML) or myeloproliferative neoplasm with a hypomethylating agent, cytotoxic chemotherapy or had undergone HSCT prior to transformation to AML were allowed and enrolled in the R/R cohort, in the appropriate arms based on venetoclax exposure.
All patients signed an informed consent; the study was approved by the Institutional Review Board and conducted in accordance with the Declaration of Helsinki.

sec.title: Treatment plan:
sec.p: The phase 1b dose finding portion used a 3+3 Bayesian algorithm for dose de-escalation to identify the maximum tolerated dose (MTD) of magrolimab, and patients were recruited in cohort sizes of 6 (Protocol Section 5.1 and 5.2). The starting dose (dose level “0”) was azacitidine 75 mg/m2 on Days 1–7 (intravenously/subcutaneously), venetoclax 400 mg/day on days 1–28 (but could be stopped after 21 days if a bone marrow [BM] documented <5% blasts or hypoplasia/aplasia at that time-point) and magrolimab in ramp-up doses as follows: 1 mg/kg dose on days 1 and 4, 15 mg/kg on day 8, 30 mg/kg on day 11, 15 and 22, followed by 30 mg/kg weekly for cycle 2, then 30 mg/kg every 2 weeks cycle 3 and beyond. Magrolimab administration was not allowed on consecutive days and hemoglobin had to be documented at >/=8.5 g/dL on the day of administration prior to the first through fourth doses of magrolimab in cycle 1 (Figure S1).

Patients were administered antimicrobial prophylaxis (including recommendation for mold active antifungals) and venetoclax dose modification were as per venetoclax USPI19. Therapy was to be continued till disease progression, significant toxicity or HSCT.

sec.title: Study assessments
sec.p: All patients had a baseline BM that included morphological, cytogenetic assessment and an 81-panel in-house CLIA certified myeloid gene mutational assessment that included TP53 as previously published20. Biallelic TP53 mutation was defined as the presence of ≥ 1 mutation, 1 mutation + an allelic loss of TP53 detected through cytogenetic assessment (karyotyping of florescent in situ hybridization) or a single TP53 mutation with a VAF≥50%.

Follow up BM assessments were done on cycle 1 between day 21–28 and after every subsequent cycle till remission, thereafter at least every 3 cycles or at progression. Measurable residual disease (MRD) assessment was done through multi-color flow cytometry (MFC) with a lower level of detection (LLOD) of 10−4 as published by our group21. Responses were tabulated as per the ELN 201718. Samples for correlative analysis were collected at baseline, at response and progression, and, while on treatment when possible (Protocol, Section 8).

sec.title: Outcome Measures
sec.p: The primary objective of the phase 1b was determination of the RP2D/MTD and safety. The primary phase 2 outcome measure was efficacy determined as composite complete response (CRc; CRc=CR+CRi) within 3 months of treatment initiation, assessed separately for the frontline and R/R cohorts. Key secondary endpoints included overall response rates (ORR) [CRc + morphological leukemia free state (MLFS)] within 3 months of therapy initiation, event free survival (EFS), overall survival (OS) and MRD response. Exploratory objectives included genomic interrogation of factors associated with response and resistance to AVM by profiling baseline and longitudinal samples with next generation sequencing (NGS) and scRNA sequencing.

sec.title: scRNA sequencing data processing
sec.p: Longitudinal BM samples from 11 treatment-naïve AML patients with TP53 mutations who were enrolled on the study were selected based on sample availability and undergone single cell RNA (scRNA) profiling using the 5’ 10XGenomics scRNA pipeline. Following library preparations, raw scRNA-seq data were demultiplexed and aligned to reference genome. Read count matrix was generated using the Cell Ranger Single Cell Software Suite provided by 10x Genomics. The R package Seurat v4 (version 4.2.1)22 was used to analyze the scRNA-seq data. Detailed quality metrics included: cells with fewer than 250 genes, or 500 transcripts were excluded from subsequent analysis; cells with 10% or more of reads mapping to mitochondrial genes were removed; possible doublets were identified and removed by (i) the DoubletFinder23 (ii) identifying cells expressing markers of distinct cell types, and (iii) identifying cells exhibiting aberrantly high gene counts. Harmony package24 was applied to remove batch effects on merged samples before clustering analysis. The filtered count matrix was normalized with the NormalizeData function and was used to identify the 2000 most variable features. Cell-cycle scores (“S.Score” and “G2M.Score”) and the cell cycle phase were calculated and assigned to each cell using the CellCycleScoring function. Cell cycle effects were regressed out using the ScaleData function. Principal component analysis was performed on variable features and an elbow plot was used to assess the optimal number of principal components. UMAP layouts and nearest-neighbor graphs were generated using the top 20 components. Different resolutions for graph-based clustering were examined to determine the best number of clusters. Differentially expressed genes (DEGs) between clusters were identified by the FindAllMarkers function from Seurat. The top DEGs for each cluster and canonical marker genes for different cell types were carefully compared to assigned cell types. Particularly, for patients with nondiploid cytogenetics, large-scale copy number variation was predicted by inferCNV25. AML cells from each patient were extracted and integrated. The phenotypical cell types of AML cells were predicted by Symphony26 based on their similarity to healthy hematopoietic cells from Granja et al27.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: Results were analyzed as intent-to treat. Demographic and clinical characteristics are summarized using descriptive statistics like median or range. Kaplan Meier method was used to estimated time to event outcomes like EFS, and OS. EFS duration was calculated from the start of treatment to lack of response, disease relapse or death, whichever was earlier, and censored at last follow-up. OS was tabulated from the start of therapy to death and censored at last follow up. Safety is summarized by category, severity, and frequency by CTCAE v4.0 using frequency tables. For scRNA-seq analysis, FindAllMarkers function from the Seurat package was used to determine differentially expressed genes. Geneset enrichment analysis of Hallmark pathways were performed using the fgsea package. The HALLMARK geneset collection was downloaded from the Molecular Signature Database and the gene set activity in each cell was calculated using AUCell. All analyses were performed in R program version 4.2.1. Defaulted “P < 2 × 10−16” reported in R was used when the P-value was below this value.

sec.title: Data availability Statement
sec.p: The single cell sequencing data generated in this study have been deposited in the GEO database with the accession number GSE269669. Qualified researchers may request access to individual patient-level metadata reported in this article after print publication. No identifying data will be provided. Below is the link to GEO database: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/geo/query/acc.cgi?acc=GSE269669 Further data may be available from corresponding authors on request.

sec.title: Results:
sec.p: From August 2020 to June 2023, 118 patients were screened, and 110 treated (Figure 1). Six patients with R/R AML were treated on the phase 1b and 104 patients on the phase 2 portion. No patient in phase 1b had any dose limiting toxicity (DLT), hence dose level ‘0” was selected as the RP2D and studied in phase 2. Thus the 6 R/R patients treated in phase 1b are combined with the R/R phase 2 cohort for aggregate efficacy and safety reporting.

sec.title: Frontline cohort
sec.p: Fifty-four patients, median age 70.1 years (range, 33 – 86) were treated in the frontline cohort (Table 1). Thirty-five patients (64.8%) had a TP53mut; 27 patients (77% of TP53mut) with biallelic/biallelic equivalent aberration. In the TP53mut cohort the median age was 67 years (range, 38–86 years); 17 patients (48.6%) had de novo AML, 11 patients (31.4%) had therapy-related AML and 7 patients (20%) had secondary-untreated AML (all prior MDS). Most patients in the TP53mut cohort had a complex karyotype (CK) [31/35 patients (88.2%)] and 32/35 patients (91.4%) had adverse-risk (AR) cytogenetics.

There were 19 patients in the TP53 wild-type (TP53wt) cohort, median age 75 years (range, 33- 83 years) including 12 patients (63.1%) with de novo AML, 2 patients (10.5%) with therapy-related AML and 5 patients (26.4%) with secondary-untreated AML (4 prior MDS and one prior CMML). 15/19 patients (78.9%) in the TP53wt cohort had ELN-AR disease.

Patients received a median of 3 treatment cycles (range, 1–33; IQR, 2–4), similar in the TP53 mutated and wild-type cohorts. A best response of CRc was noted in 34/54 patients (63%), including CR in 25 patients (46%) (Figure 2). CRc was attained in 17/35 patients (49%) in the TP53mut group (CR= 14/35, 40%; CRi=3/35=9%) and 17 patients (90%) in the TP53wt group (CR=14/19, 74%; CRi=3/19, 16%). Stratifying further, a CRc was attained in 10/27 (37%) patients with a biallelic-TP53 aberration and 7/8 (87%) patients with monoallelic-TP53 aberrations. The median time to CRc in the full cohort was 1.4 months (range 0.7–5.9 months). When stratified by the TP53 status, the median time to CRc was 1.5 months (range 0.7–5.9 months) in the TP53mut cohort and 1.4 months (range 0.7–3.7 months) in the TP53wt cohort. Four patients died before assessment of best response status (i.e., before end of cycle 2); 2 of them had persistent disease, one had an aplastic bone marrow, and one died before any BM response evaluation; all of them had TP53mut AML and are included as non-responders (NR).

At a median follow-up of 27.9 months (95% CI, 17.7–33.9 months), the median EFS and OS for the full frontline cohort (n=54) were 6.6 months (95% CI, 4.7–9.7 months) and 9.8 months (95% CI, 6.6–12.7 months), respectively (Figures 3A and 3B). In the TP53mut cohort (n=35), the median EFS and OS were 5.9 months (95% CI, 3.3–8.1 months) and 7.6 months (5.4–11.5 months) respectively with a median follow-up of 27.4 months (95% CI, 14.9–34.1 months) (Figures 3C and 3D). In the TP53wt cohort (n=19), the median EFS and OS were 9.6 months (95% CI, 5.2- 17.4) and 13 months (95% CI, 6.4–24.8) respectively, with a median follow-up of 29.7 months (95% CI, 10.9–35.4 months) (Figures 3E and 3F). The median duration of CRc was 9.1 months (95% CI, 5.9–13.6) for the full cohort; 9.2 months (95% CI, 3.8-NR) in the TP53mut and 9 months (95% CI, 4.5- NR) in the TP53wt cohort. The median EFS and OS were 4.3 months and 6.8 months for patients with biallelic-TP53, while they were 7.8 months and 10.8 months for patients with monoallelic-TP53, respectively. When combining the TP53wt and monoallelic-TP53 the median EFS were 8.5 months and 11.2 months respectively (Figure S2).

Overall 22/34 patients with CRc had a best response of MFC MRD negativity [12/17 (71%) in TP53mut and 10/17 (59%) in TP53wt cohort]; the median duration of response (DOR) was 9.2 months and OS 15.9 months in MRD negative patients; similar to the median DOR (6.7 months, p=0.44) and OS (10.8 months, p=0.35) in patients with MRD positive responses. Amongst the 12 patients in the TP53mut cohort who attained MFC MRD negativity, 4 (33%) also cleared their TP53 mutation by NGS (LLOD of about 2.0%).

Cumulatively 17/54 frontline patients (31.5%) underwent HSCT, including 12/35 (34.3%) TP53mut patients and 5/19 (26.3%) TP53wt patient (Table S1). A best response of CRc was attained in 16/17 patients prior to HSCT (one TP53mut patient had MLFS), and 12 (70.6%) were MRD negative. Five patients relapsed post HSCT (4 TP53mut and 1 TP53wt). The median follow-up of the transplanted patients was 27.9 months with a median EFS of 11 months (95% CI 5.8- NR) and OS of 16.7 months (95% CI 8.5-NR) (Figure S2). The median EFS of the transplanted TP53mut patients was 12.1 months, while OS was not reached, with 1- and 2-year OS rates of 73% and 51%, respectively (Figure S3–S4). Outcomes of patients stratified by disease ontogeny (de novo AML, secondary untreated AML and therapy related AML) and TP53 status is in Table S2.

sec.title: Post MPN-AML
sec.p: There were 4 previously untreated post-MPN AML and being reported separately. The median age of the patients at AML diagnosis was 61 years (range, 50–73 years) (Table S3). Two patients transformed to AML after an essential thrombocytosis (ET) diagnosis and 2 patients after primary myelofibrosis (MF). One patient attained a CR with a DOR of 4.4 months. The median OS was 5.9 (range 2.8–7.8) months.

sec.title: Relapsed/refractory and treated secondary AML cohort
sec.p: Fifty-two patients, median age of 61 years (range, 28 – 80 years), were treated in this cohort, which included 39 patients (75%) with R/R disease and 13 patients (25%) with treated-secondary (TS) AML (Table 2). The median prior lines of therapy were 1 (range, 1–2). Twenty-four patients (46%) had prior venetoclax (21/38 patients [53%] with R/R AML and 3/13 [23%] with TS-AML] and 12 (23%) patients had ≥1 prior HSCT. Thirty-four patients (65%) had a TP53mut (24/38 patients [63%] in the R/R cohort and 10/14 [71%] in the TS-AML cohort). In the full R/ R cohort the CRc rate was 28.9% (15 of 52 patients) and median OS was 3.9 months. The response and survival were further annotated based on prior venetoclax exposure.

In the venetoclax exposed group (n=24), CRc was attained in 4 patients (17%; CR=2, CRi=2) (Figure S5); amongst them only one patient was TP53mut, though all 4 patients had AR cytogenetics. In the venetoclax naïve group (n=28), a CRc was attained in 11 patients (39.3%, CR=9, CRi=2). The median treatment cycles were 2 (range, 1–22). At a median follow up of 16 months (95% C.I. 11.0–31.1 months), the median EFS and OS for the whole cohort was 2.3 months (95% C.I. 1.7–3.0 months) and 3.9 months (95% C.I. 3.0–5.6 months) respectively (Figure S6); the median EFS and OS was 2.2 months (95% C.I. 1.8–3.0 months) and 3.5 months (95% C.I. 2.4–4.5 months) for the venetoclax exposed group, while it was 3.5 months (95% C.I. 2.4–4.4 months) and 4.1 months (95% C.I. 2.7–9.1 months) for the venetoclax naïve group, respectively (Figure S7). The median DOR of the 15 patients who attained a CRc was 13.1 months (95% C.I. 2.5-NR). Overall, 7 patients (13.5%) could undergo a consolidative HSCT all of whom were venetoclax naïve and had attained a CRc; median OS was 12 months among transplanted patients.

sec.title: Toxicity
sec.p: There were no treatment related deaths in the 110 patients. In the phase 1b, there were no DLTs at the dose level “0” and this was chosen as the RP2D. Twenty-one patients (19%) had a grade 5 treatment emergent AE (TEAE), with 6 patients being on therapy and the remainder in the follow-up period. Majority of these events (15/21 [71.4%]) were infection related (Table 3A). Infection related complications were the most common grade 3–5 TEAEs overall (83 patients, 75.4%), which included (not exclusively) febrile neutropenia in 48 patients (43.6%), lung infection in 40 patients (35.4%), sepsis/bacteremia in 20 patients (18.2%) as the most common infections. Table 3B shows the non-infectious and non-hematological grade 3–5 AEs on the study, irrespective of attribution. There were no documented immunological toxicities with magrolimab. Twelve patients (10.9%) had an infusion reaction with magrolimab, all grade 1–2.

We captured the incidence of anemia and packed red blood cell (PRBC) transfusion requirement in the frontline cohort of 54 patients. The mean hemoglobin pre-first dose of magrolimab was 8.5 (±0.74) g/dl compared to 7.8 (±1.25) g/dl post first infusion (p=0.002); the mean hemoglobin pre and post the second dose of magrolimab were 8.8 (±0.76) g/dl and 8.8 (±0.87) g/dl (p=0.80). The median units of PRBC transfused were 6 (range 2–13) in the first 2 weeks, and 4 units (range 0–10) in the next 2 weeks. Six patients (11.5%) had hemoglobin drop >2g/dl after the first magrolimab infusion while there were no patients with same quanta of hemoglobin drop after the second infusion (Table S4). The median duration of cycle 1 was 40 days (range 31–111 days).

sec.title: Single Cell Longitudinal Profiling of TP53-mutated Patients treated with azacitidine, venetoclax and magrolimab triplet therapy
sec.p: To understand the mechanisms of response and resistance to AVM we performed scRNA-sequencing (scRNAseq) on 27 samples (11 pre- and 16 post-treatment) from 11 TP53mut patients enrolled on the frontline cohort of this study (Figure 4A). Details of demographic, clinical and molecular features of the scRNAseq patient cohort are summarized in Table S5 and Figure S8A. The correlative cohort had a median age of 63 years at diagnosis (range 59–73 years), 8 patients were responders, of which 4 went on to receive HSCT. Patients showed a diverse mutational profile with each patient carrying 2–7 distinct mutations.

After quality control and filtering of low-quality cells, 179,142 single cells were retained for further analysis (see Methods and Figure S8 B–H). Eight clusters were determined by canonical marker genes: AML, progenitors, T cells, B/Plasma cells, natural killer (NK) cells, myeloid cells, erythroid cells, and plasmacytoid dendritic cells (pDC) (Figure 4B–D). Identification of AML cells was further validated by infer copy number variation (inferCNV) and MFC as described in previous studies28. InferCNV successfully recapitulated the conventional cytogenetics of patients, and the proportions of AML cells identified in scRNA were positively correlated with the proportion of AML cells determined by clinical MFC (r=0.6, p=0.0011) (Figure S8 I–J). The cells showed varied distribution across samples, suggesting the intrinsic heterogeneity of the cellular composition in AML patients (Figure S8D). The non-AML compartment was mainly composed of T cells (Figure S9A).

sec.title: Inflammation and erythroid differentiation correlate with therapeutic resistance in TP53 mutated AML
sec.p: We first assessed differences in cellular states at baseline for responders and NR. Gene set enrichment analysis (GSEA) revealed upregulation of pathways relating to inflammation and cellular signaling in NR at baseline, including IFNγ and TNFα signaling as well as heme metabolism, suggesting that inflammatory signaling is associated with the lack of response in the context of AVM (Figure 4E; Figure S9B). Interestingly, the responders had higher LSC17 score compared to NR, a marker for leukemic stemness and relapse29(Figure S9C). This indicated that the leukemia stem cells may be susceptible to CD47 inhibition, as has been shown previously30. However, patients at relapse showed elevated LSC17 score and CD47 expression compared to those at baseline, suggesting a potential immune escape for the stem cell population (Figure 4F; Figure S9D). Additionally, patients at relapse also demonstrated enrichment of regeneration enriched cells (RECs; Figure S9E), which is a functional catalyst contributing to the non-canonical framework of AML regeneration31.

To investigate the shifts in cellular composition and the diversity of AML cells, we leveraged Symphony26 and projected AML cells onto an independent healthy reference based on their transcriptional similarity, resulting in nine distinct cellular states. Of note, NR were more likely to have erythroid differentiation enrichment post therapy, consistent with the observed increased pathway activity of heme metabolism in NR (Figure 4G; Figure S9 F–G)32. Furthermore, erythroid differentiation was also observed at the time of relapse (Figure 4G; Figure 9G). To further validate this finding, we assessed the percentage of pronormoblasts in the full frontline cohort (n=54). We observed increased pronormoblasts in TP53mut patients post treatment, supporting the association between erythroid differentiation and TP53 mutation status (Figure 4H). These findings suggest that erythroid differentiated state of AML is associated with TP53mut state post-treatment and confer resistance to azacitidine, venetoclax and magrolimab combination.

sec.title: Tumor microenvironment demonstrated factors that contributed to lack of response
sec.p: We next analyzed the AML BM microenvironment before and during treatment with AVM. While there was no difference in absolute T cell proportions across response groups (Figure S10A), we observed increased proportion of gamma-delta T cells in NR (Figure 5A; Figure S10B). Furthermore, patients with higher exhausted and senescence signatures in CD8 effector cells were less likely to respond to AVM therapy, supporting a role of T cells in response to AVM (Figure 5B).

Non-malignant myeloid cells are the main effectors of anti-CD47 based immunotherapy. We identified five non-malignant myeloid populations: two macrophage populations, CD14+ monocytes, CD16+ monocytes, and conventional dendritic cells (cDC) (Figure 5C). At baseline, non-malignant myeloid cells with an oxidative phosphorylation phenotype were strongly enriched in responders compared to NR (Figure 5D). Given that magrolimab works, in part through phagocytic induction we investigated phagocytic pathways in the nonmalignant myeloid cells. Responders showed increased phagocytosis recognition at baseline, which was maintained after AVM therapy. Lastly, given the critical role that myeloid cells play in antigen presentation we investigated whether AVM therapy affected antigen presentation pathways. HLA1 and HLA2 were higher in responders than NR both at baseline and post therapy (Figure 5E). For two patients that relapsed, we observed the loss of phagocytotic and antigen presentation activities at time of relapse compared to that at remission (Figure 5F; Figure S10C). This data supports on-target mechanism of magrolimab in contributing to responses seen to AVM therapy through both phagocytosis and increased antigen presentation.

sec.title: Discussion:
sec.p: The improvements in AML therapy have failed to significantly change the outcomes of certain genomic high-risk genomic subtypes of the disease. Studying new drugs and combinations with novel mechanisms of action is much needed in these challenging disease subsets. The study population was representative of high-risk AML in general (Table S6).

In this manuscript we present the safety, efficacy, and translational correlatives from the phase 1b/2 data of the combination of AVM in patients with newly diagnosed and R/R AML. The regimen was reasonably well tolerated with no concerning AE signal with close monitoring and infection prophylaxis. The dose expansion phase did not incur any DLTs. Through the average drop in hemoglobin was not significant after magrolimab, 11% frontline patients had >2 g/dl drop in hemoglobin after first magrolimab infusion, but none after later infusions. This warrants careful attention and monitoring and could be addressed by maintaining higher pre-first dose hemoglobin (>8.5 g/dl). There were no immunological toxicities with magrolimab, and grade 5 AEs were assessed as not related to magrolimab.

The trial included extremely high-risk patients; even in the TP53wt cohort (n=19), 6 patients (31.6%) had adverse cytogenetics, and another 9 patients had adverse mutations (ASXL1 and/or RUNX1), leading to 15/19 TP53wt patients (78.9%) being ELN2017-AR. Also, in the TP53mut cohort, 77% patients had a biallelic aberration and 88% patients had CK reflecting that these were AMLs with high-burden TP53 aberrations. The phase 1b data of hypomethylating agent + venetoclax in treatment naïve elderly patients did not report on the frequency of ELN-AR subtype but 49% of the patients had AR cytogenetics33. Notwithstanding these baseline variables, the overall clinical efficacy was modest; though the rates of CRc and CR appeared promising in the TP53mut group (49% and 43% respectively), this did not translate into durable remissions (median DOR of 9.2 months) and led to a median OS of 7.6 months. Similarly, despite a promising CRc rate of 89.5% in the TP53wt cohort median OS was 13 months. Seventeen patients (31.5%) could be consolidated with an HSCT with an OS of 16.7 months in transplanted patients and a reasonable median OS of 12.1 months in those with TP53mut. Responses and survival in patients with TS-AML and R/R AML were overall dismal and patients could not be consolidated with an HSCT given low-response rates and short DORs in responders. In the randomized setting the phase 3 ENHANCE-3 trial showed the magrolimab arm failed to show any improvement in response rates and OS compared to the placebo arm with higher fatal AEs and AE related mortality, leading to the early stopping of the trial16.

To understand the mechanisms of response and resistance to AVM, particularly in the TP53mut cohort, we employed single-cell transcriptomic profiling of the AML cells and immune microenvironment using a longitudinal cohort of patients treated on this study. Pretreatment AML cells from NR patients were enriched in inflammatory signaling pathways, consistent with previous reports28,34. Additionally, elevated expression of LSC17 and Regen71 in patients at relapse compared to baseline indicated the co-existence of LSC and RECs, representing two distinct frameworks of AML regeneration31. We identified erythroid differentiation of TP53mut AML cells as a mechanism of resistance to AVM, which is overall consistent with erythroid leukemia universally having TP53mut and being resistant to venetoclax-based therapy35–37. In terms of the immune microenvironment, we observed increased exhaustion and senescence signatures in CD8 effector cells in NR, supporting a more advanced state of terminal differentiation of CD8 cells and potentially contributing to therapy failure. This is consistent with prior work showing CD8 T cell terminal differentiation portends worse OS38. Finally, we were able to identify on-target activity of magrolimab in regulating phagocytic pathways and antigen presentation within the non-malignant myeloid population.

The addition of magrolimab to the established backbone of azacitidine-venetoclax was a potential approach that envisaged unleashing the anti-leukemic activity of the macrophage-dendritic system and generating clinical efficacy in high-risk, including TP53mut AML. Despite the preclinical data substantiating this approach and the phase 1b data of the combination of magrolimab with azacitidine showing potential clinical activity, the triplet combination failed to show meaningful clinical benefit. It is possible that different strategies to harness macrophages (via CD47, SiRPa, CLEVER-1, etc) could enhance innate immune mediated cell death better than was achieved through magrolimab resulting hopefully in a more favorable safety-efficacy profile and should continue to be explored in the future. Though the regimen did not meaningfully improve clinical outcomes, the correlative work revealed erythroid differentiation and inflammation as potential therapeutic avenues of interest in AML, especially in TP53mut AML, thereby providing future directions for therapeutic development in this extremely difficult population.

ack.title: Acknowledgement/Funding:
ack.p: The study was funded in part by Gilead Sciences. Other grant support included University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center (CA016672), University of Texas MD Anderson SPORE (C1100632), Physician Scientist Award to Hussein Abbas. Sequencing was performed by MD Anderson’s Advanced Technology Genomics Core (ATGC), which is supported in part by NIH CCSG grant P30 CA016672 (ATGC), and NIH S10 grant 1S10OD024977-01.

sec.title: Author Disclosures:
sec.p: ND has received research funding from Astellas Pharma, AbbVie, Genentech, Daiichi Sankyo, Gilead Sciences, ImmunoGen, Pfizer, Bristol Myers Squibb, Trovagene, Servier, Novimmune, Incyte, Hanmi Pharm, Fate Therapeutics, Amgen, Kite Pharma, Novartis, Astex Pharmaceuticals, KAHR, Shattuck, Sobi, GlycoMimetics, and Trillium; has been an advisor for Astellas Pharma, AbbVie, Genentech, Daiichi Sankyo, Novartis, Jazz Pharmaceuticals, Amgen, Servier, Karyopharm Therapeutics, Trovagene, Trillium, Syndax, Gilead Sciences, Pfizer, Bristol Myers Squibb, Kite Pharma, Actinium Pharmaceuticals, Arog Pharmaceuticals, ImmunoGen, Arcellx, and Shattuck; has been a data monitoring committee member for Kartos Therapeutics and Jazz Pharmaceuticals; has been a consultant or board of directors or advisory committee member for Agios, Celgene, Sobi, and STAR Therapeutics; and has received research funding from Karyopham Therapeutics and Newave Pharmaceutical. The other authors have nor relevant disclosures. HMK has received research funding from AbbVie, Amgen, Ascentage Pharma, BMS, Daiichi Sankyo, ImmunoGen, Jazz Pharmaceuticals, and Novartis as well as honoraria from AbbVie, Amgen, Amphista Therapeutics, Ascentage Pharma, Astellas Pharma, Biologix, Curis, Ipsen, KAHR, Novartis, Pfizer, Precision Biosciences, Shenzhen TargetRx, and Takeda Oncology. MY has received research funding from DaiichiSankyo and Pfizer. CDD has been a board of directors or advisory committee member for Genmab, GSK, Kura Oncology, and Notable Labs; has received honoraria from Kura, Astellas Pharma, Bluebird Bio, Bristol Myers Squibb, Foghorn Therapeutics, Immune-Onc Therapeutics, Novartis, Takeda Oncology, Gilead Sciences, and Jazz Pharmaceuticals; is a current holder of stock options for Notable Labs; has been a consultant for AbbVie and Servier; and has received research funding from Servier, Bristol Myers Squibb, Foghorn, Immune-Onc Therapeutics, Loxo Oncology, Astex Pharmaceuticals, Cleave, and Forma. TMK has been a consultant for AbbVie, Agios, BMS, Genentech, Jazz Pharmaceuticals, Novartis, Servier, and PinotBio; has received research funding from AbbVie, BMS, Genentech, Jazz Pharmaceuticals, Pfizer, Cellenkos, Ascentage Pharma, GenFleet Therapeutics, Astellas Pharma, AstraZeneca, Amgen, Cyclacel Pharmaceuticals, Delta-Fly Pharma, Iterion Therapeutics, GlycoMimetics, and Regeneron Pharmaceuticals; and has received honoraria from Astex Pharmaceuticals. GB has received research funding from Astex Pharmaceuticals, Ryvu Therapeutics, and PTC Therapeutics; has been a board of directors or advisory committee member for Pacyclex Pharmaceuticals, Novartis, CytomX, and Bio Ascend; and has been a consultant for Catamaran Bio, AbbVie, PPD Development, Protagonist Therapeutics, and Janssen. NP has received Consultancy/Scientific Advisory Board/Speaking from Pacylex Pharmaceuticals, Astellas, Pharma US, Aplastic Anemia & MDS International Foundation, CareDx, ImmunoGen, Inc, Bristol-Myers Squibb Co., Cimeio Therapeutics AG, EUSA Pharma, Menarini Group, Blueprint Medicines, CTI BioPharma, ClearView Healthcare Partners, Novartis Pharmaceutical, Neopharm, Celgene Corporation, AbbVie Pharmaceuticals, Pharma Essentia, Curio Science, DAVA Oncology, Imedex, Intellisphere, CancerNet, Harborside Press, Karyopharm, Aptitude Health, Medscape, Magdalen Medical Publishing, Morphpsys. GCI has been a consultant for Novartis, Kura Oncology, and NuProbe and received research funding from Celgene, Kura Oncology, Syndax, Merck, Cullinan, and Novartis. GGM has received research funding from Astex Pharmaceuticals, Novartis, AbbVie, BMS, Genentech, Aprea Therapeutics, Curis, and Gilead Sciences; has been a consultant for Astex Pharmaceuticals, Acceleron Pharma, and BMS; and has received honoraria from Astex Pharmaceuticals, Acceleron Pharma, AbbVie, Novartis, Gilead Sciences, Curis, Genentech, and BMS. The other authors declare no relevant conflict of interest.

caption.p: *All 6 patients in phase 1B had relapsed/refractory AML
Response rates in the frontline cohort
*the brackets show the frequency of overall response rates
Kaplan Meier survival estimates of the study patients in the frontline cohort
Abbreviations: m, median; mos, months; NAR, number at risk
Inflammation and erythroid differentiation drive resistance in TP53-mutated AML

A. Clinical design summarizing the age, gender, response type and time to response per patient. B. Dot plot for key marker genes defining cell cluster identities. C. UMAP projection of all cells passing quality control (see Supplemental Figure 1 for QC) and cell cluster identities. D. Relative proportions of cell types by patient samples. E. Barplot for the normalized enrichment score of GSEA comparing NR and responders at baseline. Color bar indicates the p-value. F. Boxplot for the LSC17 score of two responders at baseline and relapse. G. Stacked barplot of inferred phenotypical cell proportions in pre- and post-therapy samples of 3 NR and 2 responders that relapsed. H. Trend of bone marrow pronormoblast percentage of AML patients in the full frontline cohort over time post-therapy. Trend lines depicting the percentages of pronormoblasts and blasts in bone marrow samples from AML patients (n = 54). The percentages are shown from baseline to the loss date/off date for relapsed patients, or up to the best response date for non-respondent and on-protocol patients. Each trend line represents one patient, and each dot represents an assay time point.
Extrinsic factors in tumor microenvironment affects patients’ response to combined treatment

A. Proportions of different T cell subtypes in responders and NR at baseline. B. T cell senescence, exhaustion, and dysfunction score of CD4 and CD8 effector cells in responders and NR at baseline. C. UMAP of non-malignant myeloid cells. D. Enrichment plot of oxidative phosphorylation in myeloid cells of responders compared to NR at baseline. E. Phagocytosis and HLA activities in responders and NR at baseline and post therapy. F. Fold change of signatures for HLA1, HLA2, M1, M2 phagocytotic regulation and phagocytotic recognition between samples at relapse and remission for two patients.
Baseline Characteristics of patients in the frontline cohort (n=54)

fn.p: Abbreviations: mut, mutated; wt, wild type; ECOG PS, Eastern co-operative oncology group performance status; ELN, European LeukemiaNet; CTG, cytogenetics; VAF, variant allele fraction
Included patients with or without TP53 allelic loss;

caption.p: Baseline Characteristics of patients in the relapsed/refractory and treated secondary AML cohort (n=52)

fn.p: Abbreviations: mut, mutated; wt, wild type; ECOG PS, Eastern co-operative oncology group performance status; ELN, European LeukemiaNet; CTG, cytogenetics

caption.p: Grade 3–5 infectious adverse events regardless of attribution
Grade 3–5 noninfectious and non-hematological adverse events regardless of attribution

boxed-text.p: In this phase 1b/2 clinical trial we evaluated azacitidine, venetoclax and magrolimab in patients with newly diagnosed and relapsed/refractory AML, enriched with TP53 mutation. We performed transcriptomic analysis on longitudinally drawn AML cells and immune microenvironment from a subset of patients with TP53 mutated AML treated on this trial. Enrichment of inflammatory pathways and increased functional exhaustion of CD8-effector cells were seen in non-responders. Erythroid differentiation on exposure to the triplet regimen was seen at relapse in patients who had initially responded. Activation of phagocytic signatures in responders was suggestive of functional CD47 blockade with magrolimab and diminution of this phenomenon with upregulation of CD47 was noticed at relapse. Despite the subpar clinical results, the correlative analysis sheds light on mechanisms of resistance to the triplet combination, in patients with TP53 mutated AML. Evaluation of agents that mitigate these pathways could be potential options for patients with such high-risk AML.
